speaker: interviewer
text: Los Angeles, California. I'm Ellen Baskin, and we're interviewing Agnes Kun.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll one.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll one. Sorry. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Anything else?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. You're-- you're fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just gonna-- I'm just holding balance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you want me to say this is roll two on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold. You look at her--

speaker: interviewee
text: I will.

speaker: interviewer
text: --never at the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's July 28, 1994. And I'm Ellen Baskin, in Los Angeles, talking to Agnes Kun. That's K-U-N. And Agnes, I'd like you to start by telling me where and when you were born and what your childhood was like before the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK I was born on May the 3rd, 1926, in Satu Mare, Romania, at that time. It is a town of about-- it was a town of about 60,000, 70,000 population, right on the Hungarian border, only 20 miles from the Hungarian border-- 20 kilometers actually. I was an only child. My parents-- my mother never worked outside of the home. My father was an accountant by profession. But by the time I was about eight or nine years old he went into business with two other partners. They were exporting wheat, and barley, and corn, and grains from Romania, and also like some transit commerce between other countries-- same commodities. My childhood basically was a very happy one. As an only child, I had everything. My parents probably gave me everything and more than what they could afford. They were very education-minded, especially my father. And I think one of the things was languages. My father spoke several languages fluently, like Romanian, German, French. And he thought-- and he was right-- that if your native language is Hungarian you do have to learn other languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started-- he started teaching me French, probably when I was maybe four years old-- a little bit. Then I went to German kindergarten. And that was-- German was really the second language, after my native Hungarian, that I'd learned. And later on, I learned Romanian.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in first grade, we moved to Czechoslovakia for a short period of time. My father was offered a very, very good position there with a firm-- same kind of profile that he worked before. And there I went to Hungarian elementary school. Basically, that was a different country, but it was very close by. Because where I was born, it's just in that northwestern corner of Transylvania, which was pretty close to both Hungary and Czechoslovakia at that time. I was there for second and third grade. And they sent me back for fourth grade elementary school to my native town, Satu Mare, because I had to learn Romanian. Since fifth grade-- it was middle school, and it was important that I learn the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there for one year. I lived with a family. And my parents moved back in 1936 when I finished fourth grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a Catholic school, 1936 to 1940, fifth through eighth grade. It was the best school in town. And almost 1/2 of the girls were Jewish. It was a very strict Franciscan convent school. These were the early years of my childhood where I did not experience any kind of persecution. But being Jewish, somehow, as far as I can remember, always meant being a second class citizen. It meant that you're not as good as the next person. And even as a child-- and when you look in the mirror, and you don't look different from anybody else-- but since you hear it day in and day out that Jews are not as good as the rest of the people, you do start to believe it. And I think this is mainly true in families where religion and tradition is not of utter importance. And this was like my family. My parents always considered themselves Hungarians of Jewish religion-- but Hungarian first. My mother was basically the religious one in the family. She did light candles Friday night. And she sort of kept a kosher home, which meant that we brought in maybe ham or other cold cuts. But they were not used. They were not eaten from the same dishes. It was, you know, eaten off paper or paper plates. My father believed in a independent state for Jewish people. He wasn't a active Zionist, but he considered that as the solution to Jewish problem. And he was a Jew. I guess for him, like for me, being Jewish is more a state of mind than a religion. But he was not religious at all. He-- they did go to Temple on high holidays, but only because my mother insisted on it. My father probably wouldn't have even done that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think in real traditional Jewish families, that feeling of inferiority wasn't as frequent as in families like mine, where we were assimilated, and we wanted to be like everybody else. But we couldn't, because we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: My happy childhood sort of ended in 1940 for a while. Because in 1940, northern Transylvania was attached to Hungary. Hungary was a very faithful ally of Nazi Germany. And a lot of the territories that were taken away after first world war were given back to Hungary in the '40s, starting in 1938. We-- our turn came in-- on September 5th of 1940. One of the anti-Jewish laws that affected me directly was that Jewish children were not admitted to high schools. There was a ceiling of 6%. But it was entirely up to the administration of the high school whether they wanted any Jews or not. As it happened in the Catholic school that-- where I went to, they decided that we-- excuse me-- that they will only take one in the upper classes, starting with ninth grade. And the one that they took was one of my best friends. And I think her family was the richest. Probably they had a big candy factory, and I guess they were the richest among us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the rest of us, for awhile, we thought that maybe we cannot go to school anymore. And when you're raised with the importance of learning all your life, and there you are, you're just turning 14, and maybe you can't go to school anymore, it is pretty traumatic. Even though, in retrospect, compared to what awaited us, it was no big deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: But again, I was a good student, and my-- my family knew people in our community. So I did get in a public high school as one of five Jewish girls. Actually, the first year, it was a Protestant school. But then it was taken over by a public high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in subsequent years, there were no more Jews admitted. So it was us. We were five Jewish girls, and there were two Jewish boys in a parallel class. We didn't have co-ed classes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was it. There was no-- as of 1941, there were no more Jewish kids admitted. So we were it in a student body of 400 some students. In the 40's, of course, the war was already raging all around us. And the refugees from Poland, Jewish people who managed to escape, came frequently to my parents' house. Or sometimes, we went out to the trains. And we-- I remember going with my mother and taking food for the refugees. But people who came to the house, they also were asking for food, or clothes, or financial assistance, what have you. And they started to tell us about the ghettos and about concentration camps. But we didn't know, because they didn't know, that actually these camps are death camps-- that people are being systematically murdered. We assumed, and we knew, that people die at a higher rate than in normal life, but we thought that that's due to malnutrition, lack of medical attention, inadequate shelter, and such. We had absolutely no idea that people are being murdered. We heard about early concentration camps like Dachau or Buchenwald. But we never even heard about Auschwitz or any of the camps in Poland, or occupied Poland, as it may be. In the 1940's, even though terrible things were going on, but partially because we didn't know how terrible they were, and also when you're 14, 15, 16, you pretty much have your own life. I was still a happy go lucky teenager. I had my home. I had loving parents. I had friends. I had a school that I went to. So my life was just as normal as any other teenager's. I went ice skating in the winter. I went down to the river; to the beach in the summer. We started to-- not really belong-- but have some activities-- Jewish related. Like, for instance, there was the young WIZO-- W-I-Z-O. I think it's probably Women's Zionist Organization-- something like that. And we had-- like people had done plays, and we had recitals and certain activities. Also, we-- this was probably by 1942, '43-- through our Sunday school teacher, our rabbi, we helped families where the father would be away in labor camp that was attached to the army. And there-- these were poor families. And we bought food. And we took to these poor families. And looking back at it, I think I feel a little bit ashamed. Because there we were, some spoiled little brats who never even had a broom in our hands or never did anything. And we decided to clean some of these people's houses. Because we thought that it's dirty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can still see them, sort of in a corner and looking at us like, you know, what are you doing in my house? But we thought that we're doing good. And you know, we were too young, I think, to realize that it wasn't our business if their house wasn't as spotlessly clean as we were used to houses to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: But basically, we had still a good life. It was-- it wasn't totally carefree, because things started to happen. Like there was-- I think the first year, maybe 1941, there was an article in our local newspaper where they were ridiculing Jews who went to the beach by the river. This was sort of a very nice area. We-- everybody who went there, we all had like little cottages-- not to sleep in, but just to keep things for the summer and to get dressed there. And they had like little patios with benches. And, especially us youngsters, we spent a lot of time during summer there. And our parents came out in the afternoon. And the women were playing cards. And it was like really a nice place to be. But the first year this article appeared about Jews throwing goose bones on the-- on the-- you know, streets of this little park-- and so we boycotted the place for the first year. Our parents decided that we're not going to go anymore. And they were stupid, because they needed us. Because we had to pay the Jews for the maintenance of the-- of those little cottages-- and we didn't. But I guess the second year, we kids did go back. I suppose they did apologize or something. The-- our parents never went back. They decided that they are not going to go where they're not wanted. But we had nothing else to do. Traveling was impossible, because we couldn't cross borders anymore. Jews we're not allowed-- Jews didn't have passports in the '40s. So the only traveling we could do was in the country or visiting family going to Budapest, maybe. And us kids needed something for the summer. So we did go back. But little things like this happened that made life, temporarily, at least, miserable. But basically, our life was normal. This all came to an end in March of 1944 where-- when the German Army physically occupied Hungary. Up until that time, and especially starting in 1943, we started to be very optimistic. Because as soon as the siege of Leningrad and Stalingrad was over, and tides turned around, we knew that they're going to lose the war. And every day that we still stayed in our homes, every day-- well, I'm not talking about us youngsters-- but our parents and their peers were more and more optimistic. Because there it was, 1942, 1943, 1944 started. They were losing on all fronts. And we were still in our homes. And I guess it's also human nature, because for some reason, whatever happened to my neighbor is not going to happen to me. Because for some reason, we always think that we are different from our neighbors. And that was basically true. I think the dividing line was very apparent on-- between your assimilated and your religious Jews. They-- each blamed, you know, each other for what happened. The religious Jews would say, sure. God is punishing us because you guys turned away from God, and you think you're not Jews anymore. And of course, our side would say, sure. Because you look different, you act different, you don't-- you're not integrated within your community. That's why bad things happen. And you know, we found out that nobody was right, because it was enough if you had one Jewish grandparent. I mean, you could have been born Catholic, or Protestant, the laws, most of the laws, they'd apply to you. My parents were really different types. My father was very low key, even though he was a businessman-- and a successful one. But as-- I always thought that he would be happier being maybe a writer or a poet. He loved the theater. He loved to read. He loved music. But he was, I think, down to earth enough to know that one needs money to be happy in life. He was very different from his generation of Jewish businessmen because of the fact that money was never a goal in his eyes. Money was just something that assured a good life. But he was never a slave of money. And I guess the only consolation when I think of him being dead so young was that he did have a good life. He never denied us or himself anything that he could afford to have or to give us. My mother was very outgoing. She had a wonderful sense of humor. When my friends came to our house, she really used to keep us in stitches with her stories. And she was very funny. She loved people. Her only big complaint in life was that she was never a working woman. She always envied women who was-- were working outside the home. She also liked to read a lot. And she was very-- she was a very warm, good person. She always had some poor family that she helped. Like she used to send food every week, or she really always-- she was a real giver. She always gave. She always found somebody who needed more. I guess back to 1944-- when the German army occupied Hungary, that was the end of our dreams, really. I guess in my life, the first time that I experienced real fear was walking among those tanks with the SS soldiers and others, that were lined up on our streets, with our Star of David on our outer garment. That was one of the first laws that affected us. Everybody over the age of six had to wear a Star of David on our outer garment. I will still in school. We were still going to school. I was a high school senior by that time. And there we were, walking on the streets. And we knew at that point that we are at their mercy. They had the guns. They had everything. And we knew that they could spit on us. They could shoot us. They could beat us up. It was too late for anybody to stand up. So I think this was the time when I really experienced fear. It was very unfortunate. My father came back from Budapest, Saturday, March the 18th, of 1944, one day before the German army occupied Hungary. And I'm saying it was unfortunate, because he instantly saw that the only possibility to hide would be in a capital city of Budapest-- that we will never be able to hide in a small town, because everybody knows us. But unfortunately, traveling was not possible for Jews anymore. But probably we could have even managed that. But they took-- the Germans, the Nazis, took 80 Jewish adult men as hostages in my town. And my father was one of them. They locked them up in a house that belonged to a Jewish family. And they evacuated them. And every single day, the Gestapo, the German Secret Police, would go in there, and they would threaten the hostages. That if any of the family tried to hide or escape, they would shoot the hostage immediately. They did make sure, through the Hungarian police, that the family-- we the family-- knew about this threat. At that time, in the same block where we lived, there was a Jewish hospital that was evacuated. And they brought an army-- a Hungarian army hospital in these-- instead of this Jewish hospital. And the doctors who came with this hospital, with the army, they were quartered in our street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got, at the beginning, we got two Hungarian doctors who where in the army. And then one of them moved somewhere else. And we had one.

speaker: interviewee
text: These people came from the front. And they knew that they lost the war. And they saw all terrible things that happened. And they really, in their minds, they just couldn't understand all this persecution and that-- what was going on. Because they knew that it's the end. And they're losing the war anyway. I don't know if they were all OK or how much this fact contributed to the fact that they were really nice. Because they knew it's over. But the one that we had there for permanent was a very good person. Not-- and especially his wife came down to stay with him. And this was a 24-year-old woman who we never met before and who didn't know us. But I will never forget that one day, while my father was still hostage, I had to go up to the-- the-- the center of the town for something. My mother was sending me up there. And I had this yellow Star of David, of course. And his woman offered to go with me and, you know, be my friend. Which was really a big thing at that time, because you sort of expected your schoolmates and your friends to be at your side. And some of them were. But she was a complete stranger. So she was a good person. Jews had to give up radios. Jews had telephones disconnected. But they knew what's good business for them, because our phone was disconnected. But I think we were good customers. So a few days later, they came back and they connected our phone again. When they took my father as a hostage, the Gestapo came to search our house. And they were known to plant evidence while they were doing the search. This Hungarian doctor happened to be there. He put his uniform on. He was, I don't know, a captain or something in the Hungarian Army. And he went and joined the Gestapo officer and the Hungarian policeman who done-- did the search, to make sure that they're not going to plant any evidence. So some people, you know, did have a lot of decency. Also, the radios were taken away, so we gave our radio to him. He had-- my room was on the second level. The rest of the house was on one level. We had a kitchen, and pantry, and a lot of other things in a semi-basement. And he got my room on the second level. And that's where the radio was. And my father was really addicted to listening to the news during the war. I guess my cousin and I counted once that he was listening to like 18 newscasts one day-- everything from BBC, from the British radio-- in French, German, Hungarian, sometimes even Italian, even though he didn't perfectly understand it. But from the Romanian and French, he understood enough. So for him, for my father, it was a really devastating thing not to have a radio.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember he asked this doctor once, well, what is on the news? And he said it was like the Hungarian news that was going to come. And he said, oh, I'm not listening to that. Come on up, and we can listen to BBC. So that was before he was taken hostage. So from then on, like, he were-- they were listening to the BBC.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, this man knew that they lost the war. So it was, you know, maybe he was nice anyway. But he wanted to be friendly. Anyway, he was a hostage until the ghetto started. I was-- where I'm from, high school graduation is not like you're 18, and you're alive, and you graduate high school. You have a big oral and written examination-- not only with your teachers but with a committee that comes from other schools and the Board of Education. Usually, the president of the Board of Education is all of these committees. The five of us-- the seven of us Jews, actually-- the two boys, too-- were allowed to take-- our written exams were already finished. But we took our oral exams earlier as a separate group, because we had to go into the ghetto. And that was May the 1st, of 1944, when we had our oral examinations. The ghetto started on May the 3rd of 1944, on my 18th birthday. We didn't know about it until it started. But that morning, I remember our rabbi Sunday school teacher, a young man, probably under 30 at that time, Dr. Alexander Hertzog, came to pick us up, because he was put in charge of registering people for the ghetto. So the handful of Jewish students, a handful of us that were still going to school and a few who already graduated high school a year before or a couple of years before, we went with him. And we helped him with the-- the registering of the people who came into the ghetto. As I said, it started on May the 3rd. Our turn came probably close to two weeks later, about the last people to get into this area. We lived the opposite side of the town from the ghetto area. The ghetto area was actually a very heavily Jewish-- Jewish populated area before, but there were some non-Jews there too who were evacuated. It was about a three square block area. And all the Jewish people, not only from my town, but all the surrounding smaller towns, religious farms, what have you, were all concentrated in this section. The hostages were let out before we got-- we had to go into the ghetto. I remember the Jewish community was in charge to feed the hostages. And twice a day, we would take food. There was a kitchen where they made the food. And I guess we all contributed-- families who had a-- a member there. And all the youngsters, we wanted to take-- take the food in big containers. We took the food to this house where the hostages were, just to be able to maybe to see our fathers for a minute. They didn't really let them come out and talk to us. But what happened, somebody looked out from behind a corner, and then they knew that whose daughter, or son, whose-- we were a few always out there. And then they would tell the hostage to come out just for a minute to see their daughter or their son. And I remember my father, who was always very conscious about learning, and about, you know, school-- and I guess he was very proud of me, because I was a very good student. I was usually among the first three in my grade. And I remember him asking me how-- what my grades were for high school graduation. And when I told him it was all A's, I, you know, I could see he was happy about that. Anyway, we did get in the ghetto about middle of May. And we could arrange ahead of time if we knew somebody who lived in that area to move in their house. And there was this Jewish dentist whose niece was a good friend of mine. He-- they didn't have any children. But his niece was a good friend of mine. And us and a few other families arranged to stay with them. I don't know how many people we were, but I know that 20 of us slept in their living room on the floor, on mattresses that we took. We could take in mattresses, and we could take in a few things to the ghetto. But again, you know, when you're young, and you're healthy, and you're still with your parents, it's sort of like a camping trip. You don't think a lot of it. And basically, because you have no idea what is coming, you think that, oh well. We're in the ghetto. And maybe we're going to stay here. And that was not really a very unrealistic expectation, because this was May of 1944. Actually, the Russians were in by September.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Should we break?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
